title: Real-World Recommendation Systems in Fintech: Moving Beyond Collaborative Filtering
content:
![Image description](https://dev-to-uploads.s3.amazonaws.com/uploads/articles/701lwj4az9qp6zq0dz3g.png) **Why the standard ML playbook breaks in financial services - and a practical architecture for recommendation engines that actually survive production.** If you've built a recommendation system before, you've probably started with collaborative filtering. It worked beautifully on your MovieLens dataset. The precision@K looked great. Your matrix factorization converged cleanly. You felt like you understood the problem. Then you tried to apply the same thinking to a fintech product - and watched it quietly fall apart. The interaction matrix was 99.7% empty. Half your users had touched exactly one product in three years. A new savings account launched mid-sprint and the model had no idea what to do with it. Then compliance came back and asked you to explain, in plain English, why your system recommended a high-risk investment product to a retiree. "Because users with similar embedding vectors also engaged with it" didn't go over well. Here's what we learned building and shipping these systems for financial services clients across lending, banking, and investment platforms: the failure modes were always the same, and they were structural, not incidental. Collaborative filtering's core assumptions - abundant interaction data, stable item catalogs, and no obligation to explain yourself - simply don't hold in this domain. This post makes three things concrete. First, exactly why standard approaches break in fintech and what the data actually looks like. Second, a hybrid architecture designed around fintech's real constraints rather than borrowed from streaming or e-commerce. Third, a practical blueprint - with code - that you can adapt to a production system without starting from scratch. ## Why Collaborative Filtering Breaks in Financial Services Collaborative filtering is built on a simple, powerful idea: people who behaved similarly in the past will behave similarly in the future. Given enough interaction data, that assumption holds remarkably well. The problem in fintech is that almost none of the preconditions for "enough interaction data" are ever true. ### The Data Sparsity Problem A typical Netflix user watches hundreds of titles a year. A Spotify listener streams thousands of tracks. A fintech user? They might open a savings account, apply for a credit card two years later, and glance at a mortgage calculator once. The interaction matrix is almost empty.
Domain Avg. interactions / user / year Typical matrix density
Streaming (e.g. Netflix) 300–500 1–5%
E-commerce (e.g. Amazon) 50–150 0.5–2%
Fintech (banking/investment) 3–8 < 0.1%
At sub-0.1% density, the "users like you" neighbourhood is either empty or misleadingly noisy. ### No Star Ratings - Only Implicit Signals Financial products don't get rated. You infer preference from page views, calculator interactions, application starts, completions - and, critically, from support calls and cancellations as negative signals. The danger is treating all of these as equivalent positive feedback. If a user viewed a product page and cancelled their account the next day, that's not a weak positive. It's a red flag. Standard CF pipelines that flatten all interactions to a binary "engaged / not engaged" matrix will learn to confidently recommend the products that drive churn. ### The Cold-Start Problem at Fintech Scale New users arrive with no history constantly. New products - revised savings rates, seasonal loan offers, updated insurance tiers - launch frequently. Standard CF has no mechanism to bootstrap either into the recommendation surface. ### Explainability Is Not Optional In regulated financial markets, "users like you also bought this" is not an acceptable product recommendation rationale. Under MiFID II, suitability requirements, and similar frameworks, recommending a financial product carries an obligation to demonstrate it's appropriate for that specific customer. A pure CF model cannot produce that attribution. ### Item Heterogeneity A savings account and a mortgage are both "financial products" the way a documentary and a horror film are both "movies." The similarity space is not continuous in the same intuitive way. Treating item heterogeneity as a minor preprocessing concern is how you end up recommending a high-yield investment account to someone who just opened their first basic checking account. You can measure exactly how badly your interaction matrix is working against you before you build anything: ```python import numpy as np from scipy.sparse import csr_matrix def compute_sparsity(interaction_matrix): """ Compute sparsity ratio of a user-item interaction matrix. interaction_matrix: 2D numpy array or scipy sparse matrix (rows = users, cols = items) """ if isinstance(interaction_matrix, csr_matrix): total_cells = interaction_matrix.shape[0] * interaction_matrix.shape[1] nonzero_cells = interaction_matrix.nnz else: total_cells = interaction_matrix.size nonzero_cells = np.count_nonzero(interaction_matrix) density = nonzero_cells / total_cells sparsity = 1 - density print(f"Users: {interaction_matrix.shape[0]:,}") print(f"Items: {interaction_matrix.shape[1]:,}") print(f"Interactions: {nonzero_cells:,}") print(f"Density: {density:.4%}") print(f"Sparsity: {sparsity:.4%}") return sparsity # Example: 50,000 users, 200 financial products np.random.seed(42) dense = np.zeros((50000, 200)) for user in range(50000): items = np.random.choice(200, size=np.random.randint(1, 6), replace=False) dense[user, items] = 1 compute_sparsity(dense) # Output: # Users: 50,000 # Items: 200 # Interactions: 200,341 # Density: 0.0200% ← this is your problem # Sparsity: 99.9800% ``` If your density is below 0.5%, collaborative filtering alone isn't your answer. ## Rethinking the Problem - A Framework for Fintech Recommenders Collaborative filtering asks: what do users like you engage with? The right fintech question is: _what is the right product for this user, at this life stage, with this risk profile, right now?_ That reframing changes the architecture entirely. You need three distinct layers, each responsible for a signal type the others cannot provide. **Layer 1 - Content-Based Filtering**. Encode every financial product using its structured metadata: product type, risk tier, fee structure, liquidity window, minimum balance, regulatory classification. Build dense product embeddings from these features. A new fixed-rate savings account launching today has zero interaction history - but it has a full feature vector, and the model can place it meaningfully in product space from day one. This layer is your cold-start safety net for new products. **Layer 2 - Collaborative Signal**. Where behavioral data is rich enough - high-frequency app engagement, transaction patterns, multi-product usage history - apply matrix factorization using ALS for speed at scale, or BPR for more honest handling of implicit feedback. This layer captures patterns that content features can't: that users who hold both a savings account and a trading account tend to engage with pension products at a predictable tenure threshold. It handles warm users on well-established products, and it's one voice in a larger ensemble - not the whole system. **Layer 3 - Contextual Augmentation**. The first two layers describe a user's history. The third describes their present. A large deposit this week suggests a possible life event. A first child-linked savings product signals family formation. Cross-product gaps - savings but no investment product, current account but no protection cover - create explicit recommendation opportunities that historical patterns alone will never surface. These three layers feed into a unified ranking model that scores candidates and resolves conflicts before a recommendation is served. The architecture diagram in the companion visual shows the full flow. The critical design decision: the ranking model doesn't pick a winner between layers - it treats them as features. A product scoring high on content similarity but low on collaborative signal should rank differently than one scoring moderately across all three. Because each layer produces a named, interpretable score, every recommendation can be traced to a human-readable rationale - which matters enormously in Section 6. ## Solving Cold-Start With Exploration Strategies Cold-start isn't one problem. It's two distinct failure modes that happen to share a name. ### User Cold-Start: The New Customer With No History When a user signs up, the collaborative layer has nothing to work with. You have whatever they shared during onboarding - age band, income range, stated savings goals - and that's it. The right framing is **exploration**: you're not just showing the user the best product right now, you're building enough interaction history to make good recommendations later. Those goals are genuinely in tension. A **contextual bandit** treats each recommendation slot as an arm of a multi-armed bandit problem. UCB (Upper Confidence Bound) handles this by giving a scoring bonus to products that haven't been shown often. The less data you have on a product for this user, the wider the confidence interval, and the more the bandit is willing to try it. Once enough interactions accumulate, the confidence bonus shrinks and exploitation takes over. ``` python import numpy as np def ucb_score(n_total, n_item, reward_sum, c=1.0): """ Compute UCB score for a single product recommendation slot. n_total : total interactions recorded across all products so far n_item : number of times this product has been shown to this user segment reward_sum : cumulative reward (e.g. clicks, applications) for this product c : exploration constant - higher = more exploration """ if n_item == 0: return float('inf') # Always explore a product we've never shown avg_reward = reward_sum / n_item confidence_bonus = c * np.sqrt(np.log(n_total) / n_item) return avg_reward + confidence_bonus def recommend_products(product_stats, n_total, top_k=3): """ Rank products by UCB score and return the top-k recommendations. product_stats: dict of {product_id: {"n": shown_count, "r": reward_sum}} """ scores = { pid: ucb_score(n_total, stats["n"], stats["r"]) for pid, stats in product_stats.items() } ranked = sorted(scores, key=scores.get, reverse=True) return ranked[:top_k] # --- Quick demo --- product_stats = { "savings_isa": {"n": 120, "r": 34}, # well-explored, decent reward "stocks_and_shares": {"n": 45, "r": 18}, # moderate data "junior_isa": {"n": 8, "r": 4}, # sparse - bandit will explore "lifetime_isa": {"n": 0, "r": 0}, # new product - always shown first } n_total = sum(s["n"] for s in product_stats.values()) print(recommend_products(product_stats, n_total)) # Output: ['lifetime_isa', 'junior_isa', 'stocks_and_shares'] # 'savings_isa' has the best raw reward rate but is deprioritised # in favour of under-explored products with wide confidence bounds ``` The exploration constant c is tunable. Higher values push toward exploration - useful during product launches or when onboarding users from new demographics. Before the bandit takes over, cluster new users into **behavioral archetypes** derived from your existing user base. A 28-year-old with stated goal "first home" maps to a known archetype whose reward patterns seed the bandit's priors from interaction one. ### Item Cold-Start: The New Product With No Interactions Encode the new product using its feature vector and compute cosine similarity against existing products. Bootstrap it into the catalog with a content-based score only, flagged internally as scoring_mode = "content_only". Set a graduation threshold: once the product has collected a statistically meaningful number of interactions - 50–100 is a reasonable starting point - promote it to collaborative scoring automatically. Tie the threshold to statistical confidence, not just volume. A product with 50 interactions at 40% conversion has earned collaborative scoring faster than one at 2%. ## Handling Sparsity With Implicit Feedback Modelling When you feed a fintech interaction matrix into standard matrix factorization, you're making a claim the data doesn't support: that a missing entry means a neutral rating. It doesn't. It means the user never saw the product, saw it and ignored it, or simply hasn't got there yet. The root issue is treating this as a **pointwise prediction** problem when it's actually a **ranking** problem. You don't need to know how much a user will like a pension product - you need to know whether they're more likely to engage with it than with a travel insurance add-on right now. ### BPR: Pairwise Ranking for Implicit Signals Bayesian Personalised Ranking (BPR) is built around this framing. Rather than minimising prediction error on individual scores, it optimises a pairwise objective: for a given user, items they did interact with should consistently rank above items they didn't. The training signal is a comparison - this product over that one - not a score. No star ratings required. ### Weighting Implicit Signals Not all interactions carry equal information. A page view and a completed application are both "interactions" technically, but collapsing them into the same binary signal throws away your most useful data.
Interaction type                                  Suggested weight          
Product page view 1.0
Calculator / eligibility tool use 2.0
Application started 4.0
Application completed 8.0
Support call or complaint -3.0
### Negative Sampling Strategy BPR requires negative samples - items the user didn't interact with, used to form pairwise comparisons. Default random sampling wastes your most informative signal. The most useful negatives are **popular products the user actively chose not to take**. Implement a popularity-weighted negative sampler that excludes items the user was never exposed to. This concentrates training on genuine choices rather than catalog gaps. ```python import numpy as np from lightfm import LightFM from scipy.sparse import coo_matrix # --- Build a weighted implicit interaction matrix --- raw_interactions = [ (0, "savings_isa", 1.0), (0, "stocks_account", 4.0), (1, "savings_isa", 8.0), (1, "lifetime_isa", 2.0), (1, "junior_isa", -3.0), # support call - negative signal (2, "pension_fund", 1.0), (2, "stocks_account", 2.0), (2, "savings_isa", 8.0), ] items = ["savings_isa", "stocks_account", "lifetime_isa", "junior_isa", "pension_fund"] n_users = 3 n_items = len(items) item_idx = {name: i for i, name in enumerate(items)} rows, cols, data = [], [], [] for user, item, weight in raw_interactions: rows.append(user) cols.append(item_idx[item]) data.append(weight) interaction_matrix = coo_matrix( (np.clip(data, 0, None), (rows, cols)), shape=(n_users, n_items) ) sample_weight = coo_matrix( (data, (rows, cols)), shape=(n_users, n_items) ) # WARP loss is closely related to BPR but samples negatives more # aggressively - the production default for sparse fintech data model = LightFM(loss="warp", no_components=32, learning_rate=0.05, max_sampled=10) model.fit(interaction_matrix, sample_weight=sample_weight, epochs=30, num_threads=2) # Score all products for a target user target_user = 0 scores = model.predict( user_ids=np.repeat(target_user, n_items), item_ids=np.arange(n_items) ) ranked_items = [items[i] for i in np.argsort(scores)[::-1]] print(f"Ranked recommendations for user {target_user}:") for rank, item in enumerate(ranked_items, 1): print(f" {rank}. {item} (score: {scores[item_idx[item]]:.3f})") ``` WARP is the production default; treat BPR as the conceptual baseline. Track both offline metrics (NDCG, MAP) and business metrics (conversion, revenue per slot) - they will not always agree, and when they diverge, trust the latter. ## Deployment Architecture - From Notebook to Production Most recommendation system tutorials end at model training. That's where the interesting engineering begins. ### Online vs. Offline Scoring Not every user needs a real-time score. The practical answer is two parallel paths. The **cold path** runs as a nightly batch job: compute recommendations for every known user, store them in a fast key-value store (Redis, DynamoDB), and serve from cache at request time. Latency is near-zero, the model is slightly stale, and infrastructure cost is manageable. For users who log in once a day or less, this is entirely sufficient. The **warm path** activates for users showing active signals - mid-session activity, a recent large transaction, a new product eligibility change. For these users, trigger a real-time inference call against cached feature vectors. The latency budget is typically under 100ms, achievable with a lightweight re-ranking model operating on a shortlist of candidates. Routing logic can be as simple as a recency check: if the user's last event was within the past 30 minutes, take the warm path. Otherwise, serve from cache. ### The Feature Store Real-time personalisation requires a feature store - a system that serves pre-computed user features for low-latency lookup at inference time. Querying your data warehouse directly during inference breaks in production for a predictable reason: warehouse query latency is measured in seconds; inference latency must be measured in milliseconds. A feature store separates computation (done offline, on a schedule) from serving (done online, from a fast store). User risk profile, product ownership, transaction recency signals, and segment archetype all live here, updated on a defined cadence, served at sub-10ms latency. ### A/B Testing and the Counterfactual Problem Standard A/B testing assumes treatment and control groups are independent. In recommendation systems that assumption fails in a specific and consequential way: a user who was shown Product A and declined it is fundamentally different from a user who was never shown it. One has expressed a revealed preference. The other is unobserved. **Inverse Propensity Scoring (IPS)** addresses this by reweighting observed interactions by the inverse probability that they were shown under the logging policy. Items that were rarely recommended get upweighted in evaluation; ubiquitous recommendations get downweighted. The result is an unbiased estimator of how the new model would have performed had it been making recommendations all along. IPS requires logging recommendation probabilities at serving time - add that logging from day one. Retrofitting it is expensive. ## Making Recommendations Explainable for Compliance Explainability in most ML systems is a nice-to-have. In financial product recommendation, it is a legal obligation. Under MiFID II in Europe, firms making personalised financial product recommendations must demonstrate suitability - that the product is appropriate for the specific customer given their stated objectives, financial situation, and risk tolerance. Similar frameworks exist in the UK (COBS), the US (Reg BI), and Australia (best interests duty). A model that cannot produce that attribution is not just hard to audit; it may be non-compliant to deploy. The hybrid architecture earns its compliance justification here. The content-based layer is interpretable by design. Every recommendation driven primarily by product features can be traced to a human-readable rationale: "Based on your current savings balance, 3-year product tenure, and stated growth objective, this Stocks and Shares ISA aligns with your risk profile." That sentence is constructed directly from feature values, not inferred from latent space proximity. It survives an audit. The collaborative layer is harder. SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) is the practical fix: compute SHAP values on the features feeding into your ranking model and surface the top three drivers per recommendation. You won't explain the embedding directly, but you can explain what features made the ranked output what it is - typically sufficient for audit purposes. One area practitioners consistently under-weight: **fairness as a compliance risk**. If your model systematically under-recommends premium products to users in certain demographic segments - because those segments are underrepresented in training data - that is not just an ethical problem. In many jurisdictions it constitutes discriminatory financial advice. Audit your recommendation distributions across demographic cohorts before you ship, and alert on distributional drift in production. ## Lessons From the Field - What Actually Broke Every system described above looks clean in an architecture diagram. Here is what it looks like in practice. **Offline metrics lied to us**. We spent weeks optimising NDCG and MAP on our held-out test set. The numbers looked strong. Then we ran the A/B test and saw a flat conversion curve. Our test set reflected past recommendation exposure, not genuine user preference - a product that appeared in every user's feed had high recall by default. We stopped reporting NDCG for business reviews and switched to expected revenue per recommendation slot. An imperfect proxy, but one that aligned the model's objective with what the business actually cared about. **A rules-based baseline beat our best model for cold-start users**. The ML model won every offline evaluation. In production, for users with fewer than five interactions, a simple decision tree - if age 25–35 and stated goal is home ownership, recommend lifetime ISA - outperformed it on click-through and application rate. The model had too little data to learn anything meaningful and was fitting noise. We kept the ML model for warm users and ran the rules engine in parallel for cold-start. Neither camp felt great about this, which is probably a sign it was the right call. **We ignored the negative signal and paid for it**. Support calls and cancellations weren't in the training pipeline at launch. The result was a model that confidently re-recommended products users had already rejected. One product had an unusually high support call rate following recommendation - the model kept surfacing it because interaction volume was high, with no mechanism to distinguish engaged with from called to cancel. Adding signed weights to the interaction matrix fixed this, but we lost three months of clean signal first. **We shipped without a monitoring strategy**. A new product launched six weeks after go-live, similar enough in feature space to existing products that it crowded them out of top-K results for a large user segment. Cold-start performance degraded silently for two weeks. We had dashboards for ranking model output quality; we had nothing watching the item distribution of what was actually being served. Now we alert on recommendation distribution shift the same way we alert on prediction latency. **Explainability was not a post-launch task**. We planned to retrofit interpretability tooling after launch. Adding post-hoc SHAP attribution required re-engineering the serving layer, retraining with feature logging enabled, and reconciling the explanations with a compliance team that had already reviewed a different architecture. The rebuild cost more engineering time than building the hybrid architecture from day one would have. The architecture in Section 2 of this post is written with that lesson in mind. ## Conclusion Collaborative filtering isn't the wrong tool for fintech - it's an incomplete one. Its assumptions about data density, feedback explicitness, and item homogeneity simply don't hold in financial services, and building around those assumptions produces systems that look functional in evaluation and quietly underperform in production. The path to a recommendation engine that actually ships and stays healthy runs through five things: a hybrid architecture that treats content, collaborative, and contextual signals as distinct inputs rather than substitutes; cold-start strategies grounded in bandit exploration and content encoding rather than waiting for data that may never come; implicit feedback modelling with BPR or WARP instead of naïve matrix factorization; a deployment architecture with a feature store, dual scoring paths, and counterfactual evaluation built in from the start; and explainability treated as a first-class design constraint, not a compliance checkbox added after the model is already live. None of this is dramatically more complex than the standard approach. It's just more honest about the domain. _If you're curious about how these systems work in practice, the team at **Capital Numbers** has put together a solid overview of [AI-driven recommendation systems in fintech](https://www.capitalnumbers.com/blog/ai-driven-recommendation-systems-finance/) - focused on business application rather than model architecture._
